9	# Feature: DDx Server Web UI
10	
11	**ID:** FEAT-008
12	**Status:** In Progress
13	**Priority:** P1
14	**Owner:** DDx Team
15	
16	## Overview
17	
18	The DDx server (`ddx-server`) serves a web UI for browsing artifacts, beads,
19	the artifact dependency graph, and DDx run activity. Artifacts include markdown
20	documents plus sidecar-identified media such as diagrams, wireframes, images,
21	and PDFs. The UI is a SvelteKit application built with Svelte 5, embedded into
22	the Go binary via `embed.FS`, and served alongside the MCP and HTTP API
23	endpoints from a single process.
24	
25	`ddx-server` runs as a per-user host daemon (one instance per machine, see
26	FEAT-002 and FEAT-020), so the same UI enumerates every project that the
27	host+user registry knows about and binds its navigation to one selected
28	project context at a time. The node-aware URL structure and combined
29	cross-project dashboards are specified in FEAT-021; FEAT-008 owns the
30	underlying views that those routes render.
31	
32	The UI consumes GraphQL operations defined in `cli/internal/server/graphql/schema.graphql`.
33	Every page lists its required query or subscription by name for traceability.
34	
35	## Problem Statement
36	
37	**Current situation:** DDx artifacts, beads, and the artifact graph are only accessible via CLI commands or MCP tool calls. There's no visual way to browse the artifact library, see the dependency graph, check bead status, regenerate generated artifacts, or review layer-aware run evidence.
38	
39	**Pain points:**
40	- CLI output for `ddx doc graph` is text-only — dependency graphs need visual rendering
41	- Bead status and dependency trees are hard to grok in terminal output
42	- DDx run activity has no browsable interface that preserves the `work` -> `try` -> `run` layers
43	- Non-markdown artifacts have no type-aware browser preview, even when the graph knows their identity
44	- Generated artifacts have no UI affordance for checking provenance or requesting regeneration
45	- New team members can't quickly understand what artifacts exist or how they relate
46	
47	**Desired outcome:** A web dashboard served from the same `ddx-server` binary that already handles MCP and HTTP API. Developers open a browser, see their artifact library, dependency graph, bead board, generated-artifact provenance, and DDx run activity — all from one URL.
48	
49	## Architecture
50	
51	### Single Binary, Multiple Surfaces
52	
53	```
54	ddx-server binary
55	├── /            → Web UI (embedded SvelteKit)
56	├── /api/        → HTTP REST API (JSON) — frozen
57	└── /graphql     → GraphQL endpoint (gqlgen)
58	```
59	
60	The SvelteKit frontend queries the `/graphql` endpoint for all data. REST endpoints
61	remain unchanged and are consumed by the CLI and MCP tools only.
62	
63	### Project Context
64	
65	The UI is rendered under the node-scoped URL structure defined in FEAT-021:
66	`/nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/...` for project-scoped views and
67	`/nodes/:nodeId/...` for combined node-wide dashboards. The root `/`
68	redirects to `/nodes/:nodeId` using the ID returned by `GET /api/node`
69	(FEAT-020). When more than one project is registered the navigation bar
70	exposes a project picker populated from `GET /api/projects`; selecting a
71	project swaps the `:projectId` segment in place. All GraphQL queries issued by the
72	UI are bound to the selected project context.
73	
74	### Project Navigation Tabs
75	
76	Each `/nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/...` page exposes the following
77	project-scoped tabs in the global navigation, in this order:
78	
79	| Tab | Route segment | Purpose |
80	|---|---|---|
81	| Overview | (none) | Project home, recent activity, operator-prompt input (ADR-021) |
82	| Beads | `beads` | Bead list / kanban / ready / detail (US-082*) |
83	| Artifacts | `artifacts` | Artifact browser (US-080, US-081b, US-083) |
84	| Graph | `graph` | Document dependency graph (US-081, US-081c) |
85	| Runs | `runs` | Layer-aware run history with `work` / `try` / `run` chips, row expansion into the shared `work` / `try` / `run` run-detail tab strip, and re-queue (US-086b, US-086c) |
86	| Workers | `workers` | Live + recent worker processes for this project (US-085b, US-086, US-086a) |
87	| Personas | `personas` | Persona browser + role bindings (US-097) |
88	| Plugins | `plugins` | Plugin registry + install/uninstall (US-098) |
89	| Commits | `commits` | Project git commit log (US-093, FEAT-021) |
90	| Efficacy | `efficacy` | Model efficacy + comparisons (US-096) |
91	
92	**Sessions and Executions are retired as top-level activity tabs**
93	(Story 8). The unified Runs tab carries the shared layer-aware tab
94	structure and inline row expansion that surface what those tabs used to
95	show. The shared detail tabs are:
96	
97	- `work` rows: `overview`
98	- `try` rows: `overview`, `prompt`, `response`, `tools`, `evidence`
99	- `run` rows: `overview`, `prompt`, `response`, `session`, `tools`, `evidence`
100	
101	### Run-detail page
102	
103	The shared run-detail page is the canonical deep surface for anything exposed
104	through the Runs tab.
105	
106	- Trust boundary: anyone with project read access can inspect the raw prompt,
107	  response, stderr, and tool-call arguments attached to visible `try` and
108	  `run` records. The page does not redact those fields inside the project
109	  boundary.
110	- Evidence browser whitelist: the Evidence tab renders only `*.txt`, `*.md`,
111	  `manifest.json`, `prompt.md`, and `result.json` inline, and only when the
112	  file is 64 KB or smaller. Other files remain download-only. The evidence
113	  browser does not render Markdown as HTML.
114	
115	- `layer=run` rows expand to AgentSession transcript / billing / cached
116	  tokens / stderr (the old Sessions detail).
117	- `layer=try` rows expand to the `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` bundle
118	  metadata, check results, and verdict (the old Executions detail).
119	- `layer=work` rows expand to queue inputs and child run links.
120	
121	**URL deprecation policy.** During the deprecation window, the legacy
122	URLs respond with **302 redirects (NOT 301)** to the matching filtered
123	Runs URL with all query params preserved, plus a `Sunset` response
124	header that names the cutoff date for the redirects:
125	
126	| Legacy URL | Redirect target |
127	|---|---|
128	| `/sessions` | `/runs?layer=run` |
129	| `/sessions#<id>` | `/runs?layer=run&session=<id>` |
130	| `/executions[?bead=&verdict=&harness=&q=&after=]` | `/runs?layer=try` with the original query string preserved verbatim |
131	| `/executions/[id]` | `/runs/[runId]?bundle=true` |
132	
133	302 (not 301) is intentional: bookmarks and external link aggregators
134	should not cache the redirect permanently while we still own the legacy
135	routes. The redirect response carries `Sunset: <RFC 9745 date>` and the
136	project nav drops the Sessions/Executions entries once the redirects and
137	the internal-link sweep land (`shellRoutes.ts`, `BeadDetail.svelte`,
138	commits page, agent page, Workers detail).
139	
140	### Build Pipeline
141	
142	```
143	cli/internal/server/frontend/   → SvelteKit + TypeScript
144	  bun run build                 → cli/internal/server/frontend/build/
145	cli/internal/server/            → Go with embed.FS
146	  go build                      → ddx-server (single binary with embedded UI)
147	```
148	
149	During development, SvelteKit's dev server proxies `/graphql` to the running Go server.
150	
151	### Technology Choices
152	
153	| Layer | Choice | Why |
154	|-------|--------|-----|
155	| Frontend framework | **Svelte 5** | Compile-time optimizations, no Virtual DOM |
156	| Build tool | **SvelteKit** | Router, layout, API routes, hooks, adapter-static for embedding |
157	| Styling | **Tailwind CSS** | Consistent with website (Hextra uses Tailwind) |
158	| Data fetching | **graphql-request + graphql-ws** | Lightweight GraphQL client with typed queries and WebSocket subscriptions |
159	| Graph visualization | **D3.js** or **Cytoscape.js** | For document dependency graph rendering |
160	| Embedding | **Go embed.FS** | Compile frontend into the binary — no external files needed |
161	| Go HTTP | **Chi** or **net/http** | Standard library compatible routing |
162	| Go GraphQL | **gqlgen** | Schema-first, code-generated resolvers |
163	
164	### Alternatives Considered
165	
166	**Templ + htmx** — simpler (no JS toolchain), but the dependency graph visualization and interactive bead board need more interactivity than htmx provides well. Templ + htmx is great for CRUD; we need a richer graph/dashboard experience.
167	
168	**Local-first with CRDT sync** — interesting for offline use but overkill for v1. The UI is primarily read-heavy (browsing docs, viewing graphs). Can be added later with PowerSync or ElectricSQL if needed.
169	
170	## Requirements
171	
172	### Functional
173	
174	1. **Artifact browser, viewer, editor, and regeneration**
175	   - List artifacts by category (prompts, personas, patterns, templates, ADRs,
176	     SDs, diagrams, wireframes, generated images, PDFs)
177	   - Full-text search across textual artifact content, sidecar metadata, and
178	     markdown frontmatter
179	   - View markdown artifacts rendered as markdown with syntax highlighting
180	   - Render artifacts by `media_type`:
181	     - `text/markdown`: markdown renderer with syntax-highlighted code blocks
182	     - `image/svg+xml` with Mermaid provenance: sanitized inline SVG preview
183	     - `application/vnd.excalidraw+json`: Excalidraw read-only embed
184	     - `image/*`: inline image preview with metadata fallback
185	     - `application/pdf`: embedded PDF preview with open-file fallback
186	     - unknown or binary media: metadata panel plus open/download affordance
187	   - Show `ddx:` frontmatter or sidecar `.ddx.yaml` metadata (id,
188	     `media_type`, dependencies, `generated_by`, and staleness status)
189	   - Clickable dependency links — navigate upstream/downstream docs inline
190	   - **Editor**: in-browser markdown editing with save-to-disk (calls API to write file)
191	   - Sidebar showing document dependency tree for current doc
192	   - Staleness indicator per document (fresh/stale/missing-deps)
193	   - Generated-artifact provenance indicator: show producing run id, prompt or
194	     generator summary, and source-hash status when `generated_by` is present
195	   - Generated-artifact staleness is artifact-local: a `generated_by.source_hash`
196	     mismatch marks only the generated artifact stale and does not cascade like
197	     `depends_on`
198	   - Regenerate action for generated artifacts calls the `artifactRegenerate`
199	     mutation, returns the new run id, and links directly to the produced run
200	     detail and updated artifact
201	
202	2. **Document dependency graph**
203	   - Interactive visual graph (D3/Cytoscape) showing all document relationships
204	   - Color-code nodes by staleness (green=fresh, orange=stale, red=missing-deps)
205	   - Click node to navigate to document detail
206	   - Filter by document type, staleness status
207	   - Zoom/pan for large graphs
208	   - Layout options (hierarchical top-down, force-directed)
209	
210	3. **Bead views**
211	
212	   The beads UI runs entirely in the browser. On load, the client fetches the
213	   full bead set from the `beads` query (GraphQL). All search, filter, sort, and graph
214	   traversal happens client-side using an in-browser data layer.
215	
216	   **Master-detail list view:**
217	   - Searchable list: full-text across title, description, acceptance, labels
218	   - Sortable columns: ID, title, status, priority, labels, owner, updated_at
219	   - Filterable by status, priority, label, owner, and a `Needs review` lane
220	   - Click a bead to open detail panel (split pane or slide-over)
221	   - Detail shows: all fields, dependency tree, execution beads, agent sessions
222	   - Graph traversal: click a dependency → navigate to that bead's detail
223	   - Drill-down into run records linked to the bead (shows layer, pass/fail,
224	     logs, duration)
225	
226	   **Kanban board view:**
227	   - Columns represent status: `open`, `in_progress`, `closed`
228	   - Cards within columns ordered by priority (P0 top, P4 bottom)
229	   - Dependency grouping: beads that block each other cluster visually
230	     (similar to Trello, Fuzz by 37signals, GitHub Projects)
231	   - Drag-and-drop to change status (calls `beadUpdate` mutation)
232	   - Swimlanes option: group rows by label (e.g., `area:cli`, `area:web`)
233	   - Color coding: priority → card border, labels → chips, blocked → dimmed
234	   - Collapsed card shows: title, priority badge, label chips, dep count
235	   - Expanded card shows: description, acceptance, execution summary
236	
237	   **Ready queue view:**
238	   - Filtered view showing only beads with all dependencies satisfied
239	   - Sorted by priority, then by creation date
240	   - One-click claim (calls `beadClaim` mutation)
241	   - Single-ticket ready work is shown ahead of epics; open epics appear in a
242	     separate epic lane rather than the ordinary work queue
243	
244	   **Bead detail actions:**
245	
246	   The detail panel is not just a viewer — it is the primary interaction
247	   surface for evaluating and driving work items.
248	
249	   *Navigation:*
250	   - `spec-id` links to the governing artifact (rendered inline or navigates
251	     to the document viewer with that artifact's content)
252	   - Parent bead link navigates to the parent's detail
253	   - Dependency links navigate to each dep's detail
254	   - Run records link to the layer-aware run detail view (logs, results)
255	   - Agent session links navigate to the agent log viewer for that session
256	
257	   *Review and re-evaluation:*
258	   - "Review" action: side-by-side view of the bead's acceptance criteria
259	     vs the latest execution results and implementation diff. The user or
260	     agent evaluates whether the work actually meets the spec. This dispatches
261	     the TD-033 structured evidence envelope in no-tool reviewer mode so the
262	     reviewer reasons only over the assembled evidence.
263	   - "Re-review" action on closed beads: re-evaluates against current
264	     artifact state — did the governing spec change since the bead was
265	     closed? If so, flag as potentially stale.
266	   - Artifact drift indicator: if the `spec-id` artifact's content hash
267	     changed since the bead was last updated, show a warning badge.
268	
269	   *Status and lifecycle:*
270	   - Re-open a closed bead (status → open) with a reason field
271	   - Re-run bead work: dispatch `ddx try <bead>` through the server worker API,
272	     then open the resulting `try` record under the layer-aware run history
273	   - Run linked checks: when the bead references an execution definition,
274	     dispatch that definition as project checks and show the resulting record
275	     separately from bead re-run history
276	   - Claim / unclaim for agent coordination
277	   - Inline status transitions (open → in_progress → closed)
278	
279	   *Mutation:*
280	   - Edit all bead fields inline (title, description, acceptance, labels,
281	     priority, spec-id)
282	   - Create new beads with guided form
283	   - Add/remove dependencies by selecting other beads
284	   - All mutations call the HTTP API which writes to the JSONL store
285	
286	4. **Agent log monitor**
287	   - Live-updating list of agent invocations (newest first)
288	   - Columns: timestamp, harness, model, tokens, duration, exit code
289	   - Click to expand: DDx metadata, native session/trace references, and any
290	     DDx-owned detail available for that invocation
291	   - Filter by harness, time range, exit code
292	   - Token usage summary (provider-native or DDx-observed, depending on source)
293	   - Auto-refresh on configurable interval (or subscription in v2)
294	
295	5. **Layer-aware run history and detail**
296	   - List DDx run records from the unified substrate with explicit layer chips:
297	     `work`, `try`, and `run`
298	   - Filter by layer, status, harness, provider, model, bead id, artifact id,
299	     and time range without introducing a run-type catalog beyond the three
300	     layers

447	### Performance Contract: Artifact Listing
448	
449	The artifact listing view carries a provisional performance contract for design
450	work. These budgets shape the current implementation, but the gating numbers
451	stay provisional until the B7-C1 baseline measurement is recorded and published
452	in the perf docs. Treat the values below as design-time budgets, not final
453	thresholds.
454	
455	| Budget | Provisional contract | Revisit when |
456	|---|---|---|
457	| Initial load | First usable content within 2s on the reference fixture | Baseline measurement lands with different gating numbers |
458	| Search/filter latency | Interactions settle within 200ms once the current list data is already loaded | Search becomes data-loading bound instead of DOM-bound |
459	| Steady-state DOM rows | Default list view stays flat and non-virtualized at up to 500 visible artifact rows | The default state needs 500-1000 visible rows, more than 1000 visible rows, or sticky group headers |
460	
461	- Revisit the contract before shipping if the artifact listing design adds any
462	  of the following:
463	  - infinite scroll
464	  - auto-prefetch
465	  - 500-1000 visible DOM rows in the default list state
466	  - more than 1000 visible DOM rows in the default list state
467	  - sticky group headers
468	- The default list view assumes a flat, non-virtualized DOM. If the design
469	  changes that assumption, update this section and the TP-002 measurement
470	  methodology together.
471	- Story 5 is page-local unless we explicitly add server-side `groupBy`: the
472	  browser groups only the loaded artifact edges. Story 5 owners should treat
473	  any requirement for cross-page or corpus-wide grouping accuracy as the
474	  trigger to move `groupBy` into the GraphQL resolver and make it server-side.
475	- Story 6 search/filter stays server-side: any query that changes result
476	  membership (`q`, `mediaType`, `staleness`, `phase`, `prefix`, `sort`) must
477	  continue to round-trip through the resolver so pagination and bookmarks stay
478	  correct. Story 6 owners should treat server-side filtering as mandatory, not
479	  optional, for any corpus-wide search or filter UI.
480	- Add server-side `groupBy` when group headers must reflect the full corpus,
481	  grouping needs to stay correct across page boundaries, or the page-local
482	  grouping pass can no longer represent the loaded set faithfully.
483	- Add virtualization when the visible artifact list needs more than the flat
484	  500-row DOM budget, or when scroll cost is dominated by DOM rendering rather
485	  than data loading.
486	
487	### Non-Functional
488	
489	- **Performance:** Initial page load <2s. API calls <200ms. Graph renders smoothly for 100+ nodes.
490	- **Deployment:** Single binary. No external runtime dependencies. No separate frontend server in production.
491	- **Development:** `bun run dev` proxies to Go server. Hot reload for frontend. `go run` for backend.
492	- **Responsive:** Usable on tablet/laptop screens. Not optimized for mobile.

1268	## Implementation Notes

1270	### Embedding Pattern (Pocketbase-style)
1271	
1272	```go
1273	//go:embed all:frontend/build
1274	var frontendFiles embed.FS
1275	
1276	func main() {
1277	    distFS, _ := fs.Sub(frontendFiles, "frontend/build") // relative to embed.go in cli/internal/server/
1278	    
1279	    mux := http.NewServeMux()
1280	    mux.Handle("/api/", apiRouter)
1281	    mux.Handle("/mcp/", mcpHandler)
1282	    mux.Handle("/", spaHandler(http.FS(distFS))) // SPA fallback
1283	}
1284	
1285	// spaHandler serves static files, falling back to index.html for SPA routing
1286	func spaHandler(fs http.FileSystem) http.Handler {
1287	    fileServer := http.FileServer(fs)
1288	    return http.HandlerFunc(func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
1289	        if _, err := fs.Open(r.URL.Path); err != nil {
1290	            r.URL.Path = "/"
1291	        }
1292	        fileServer.ServeHTTP(w, r)
1293	    })
1294	}
1295	```

8	# Feature Specification: FEAT-008 — Artifact Template Quality and Completeness
9	
10	**Feature ID**: FEAT-008
11	**Status**: Draft
12	**Priority**: P1
13	**Owner**: HELIX maintainers
14	
15	## Overview
16	
17	HELIX artifact templates (Vision, PRD, Feature Spec, Solution Design,
18	Technical Design, ADR, Test Plan) should be self-documenting and
19	machine-interpretable. Each template must include clear instructions,
20	agent-oriented generation prompts, review checklists, and relationship
21	declarations that allow the artifact graph to be constructed automatically.
22	
23	## Problem Statement
24	
25	- **Current situation**: Templates exist under
26	  `.ddx/plugins/helix/workflows/phases/*/artifacts/*/template.md` but vary
27	  in completeness. Some include generation prompts, others don't. Review
28	  checklists are absent. Relationship declarations are embedded in meta.yml
29	  but not surfaced to agents or reviewers.
30	- **Pain points**:
31	  1. Agents produce artifacts with missing or weak sections because templates
32	     don't explain what belongs in each section.
33	  2. Adversarial review has no structured checklist, so reviewers apply
34	     inconsistent criteria.
35	  3. The artifact graph (Vision → PRD → Specs → Designs → Tests) cannot be
36	     constructed automatically because relationship declarations are
37	     inconsistent across templates.
38	  4. New HELIX users don't know what a "good" artifact looks like because
39	     templates lack examples.
40	
41	## Functional Requirements
42	
43	### FR-1: Section Instructions
44	
45	Each artifact template must include a brief instruction block (HTML comment
46	or frontmatter annotation) for every section, explaining what belongs there
47	and what does not.
48	
49	### FR-2: Generation Prompts
50	
51	Each artifact template must include or reference a generation prompt that
52	agents can use to draft the artifact from higher-level governing artifacts.
53	The prompt must specify which upstream artifacts to read and what context to
54	synthesize.
55	
56	### FR-3: Review Checklists
57	
58	Each artifact template must include a review checklist that adversarial
59	review agents use to evaluate the artifact. The checklist must be specific
60	to the artifact type (e.g., "Does the PRD define success metrics?" rather
61	than generic "Is this complete?").
62	
63	### FR-4: Relationship Declarations
64	
65	Each artifact template must declare its upstream dependencies and downstream
66	artifacts in a machine-readable format (frontmatter `depends_on` and
67	`enables` fields), so the artifact graph can be traversed programmatically.
68	
69	### FR-5: Exemplar Artifacts
70	
71	Each artifact type must include at least one exemplar — a fully worked
72	example of a well-written artifact — either inline in the template or as a
73	companion file. Exemplars should be drawn from real HELIX projects where
74	possible.
75	
76	## Non-Functional Requirements
77	
78	### NFR-1: Template Size
79	
80	Templates including instructions and checklists must remain under 200 lines
81	to avoid overwhelming agents with boilerplate.
82	
83	### NFR-2: Backward Compatibility
84	
85	Existing artifacts must remain valid. New template features are additive.
86	
87	## Acceptance Criteria
88	
89	1. Every artifact template under `workflows/phases/*/artifacts/*/template.md`
90	   includes section instructions, a generation prompt reference, and a review
91	   checklist.
92	2. Every artifact template declares `depends_on` and `enables` relationships
93	   in its meta.yml.
94	3. At least one exemplar artifact exists for each artifact type.
95	4. `helix review` can load the review checklist for the artifact type being
96	   reviewed and evaluate against it.
97	
98	## Constraints
99	
100	- Templates must work with the existing DDX artifact management system.
101	- Relationship declarations must be compatible with the existing `ddx`
102	  frontmatter format.
103	
104	## Out of Scope
105	
106	- Automated artifact graph visualization (future work).
107	- Enforcing template compliance at write time (advisory only for now).
108

25	func TestGraphQLPerfMatrix_Baseline(t *testing.T) {
26		if testing.Short() {
27			t.Skip("perf matrix skipped in -short mode")
28		}
29		spec := DefaultBeadFixtureSpec()
30		f := BuildBeadFixture(t, spec)
31		report := RunMatrix(t, f, DefaultIterations)
32	
33		// Race detector overhead (5-10x sync cost) invalidates microbenchmark
34		// percentile budgets. Under -race we still want the matrix to run and
35		// log the measurements, but the strict p95 gates are skipped — they are
36		// meaningful only in non-race builds. Lefthook's full-tests runs `go
37		// test -race -cover ./...`, which would otherwise gate pushes on a
38		// detector artefact rather than a real regression.
39		enforceBudgets := !raceEnabled
40		for _, r := range report.Targets {
41			switch r.Name {
42			case "bead":
43				if enforceBudgets && r.InProcess.P95 > 50 {
44					t.Errorf("bead(id:) in-process p95=%.2fms > 50ms budget", r.InProcess.P95)
45				}
46				if enforceBudgets && r.HTTP != nil && r.HTTP.P95 > 200 {
47					t.Errorf("bead(id:) HTTP p95=%.2fms > 200ms budget", r.HTTP.P95)
48				}
49			case "beadsByProject":
50				if enforceBudgets && r.InProcess.P95 > 75 {
51					t.Errorf("beadsByProject in-process p95=%.2fms > 75ms budget", r.InProcess.P95)
52				}
53				if enforceBudgets && r.HTTP != nil && r.HTTP.P95 > 250 {
54					t.Errorf("beadsByProject HTTP p95=%.2fms > 250ms budget", r.HTTP.P95)
55				}
56			}
57			httpLine := "n/a"
58			if r.HTTP != nil {
59				httpLine = fmt.Sprintf("p50=%.2fms p95=%.2fms p99=%.2fms", r.HTTP.P50, r.HTTP.P95, r.HTTP.P99)
60			}
61			t.Logf("target=%s iter=%d in-proc(p50=%.2fms p95=%.2fms p99=%.2fms) http(%s)",
62				r.Name, r.Iterations,
63				r.InProcess.P50, r.InProcess.P95, r.InProcess.P99,
64				httpLine,
65			)
66		}
67	
68		// Optional report writing — keeps CI quick by default, lets humans
69		// regenerate the baseline with `DDX_BENCH_REPORT_DIR=... go test ...`.
70		if dir := os.Getenv("DDX_BENCH_REPORT_DIR"); dir != "" {
71			date := os.Getenv("DDX_BENCH_REPORT_DATE")
72			if date == "" {
73				date = time.Now().UTC().Format("2006-01-02")

456	|---|---|---|

data.output:
ID:       ddx-9ce6842a
Title:    Bead list and cross-project all-beads are too slow: establish a GraphQL perf harness and fix N·M scan pattern
Type:     task
Status:   closed
Priority: 1
Labels:   feat-008, feat-004, perf, graphql
Desc:     ## Observed

Per-project `/beads` page is noticeably slow; the cross-project "all beads" view is substantially slower. Related: `ddx-ad0db8fd` already identified the single-bead-detail slowness caused by the same storage pattern.

## Root cause (same pattern, different call site)

`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_beads.go:22,40` —

- `Beads` (cross-project) and `BeadsByProject` (single-project) both call `r.State.GetBeadSnapshots(status, label, projectID, search)`.
- `GetBeadSnapshots` (`cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go:49-105`) iterates every registered project, constructs a `bead.Store` per project, calls `ReadAll()` (scan + JSONL parse of every bead file), filters in Go. Cost is `O(projects × beads_per_project)` on every call, independent of the filter/page size.
- All filtering (status, label, project, search) is Go-side, post-read. The page-size window is applied in `beadConnectionFromSnapshots` after the full corpus is already materialized.
- Cross-project "all beads" = same loop, just without the projectID filter. It is the worst case by design.

This is the same shape that's breaking the Sessions feed (`ddx-2ceb02fa`) and single-bead detail (`ddx-ad0db8fd`). A point fix here is insufficient — we need a measurement surface first so we stop relying on anecdote.

## Proposed direction

### Part 1 — Perf harness (foundational; enables everything else)

Introduce a reusable GraphQL perf harness under `cli/internal/server/perf/` or `cli/internal/server/graphql/bench_test.go`:

- Seeded fixture builder: generates N projects × M beads with configurable distribution of status/labels/dependencies. Reuses existing store primitives (no fake data layer).
- Go benchmark + scriptable HTTP runner (wrapper around an already-running server) reporting p50/p95/p99 per query shape.
- Targets covered on day one: `bead(id:)`, `beadsByProject(projectID, first, after, status, label, search)`, `beads(projectID=nil, first, after, status, label)`, `docGraph`, `sessions` (so the sessions shard work `ddx-2ceb02fa` inherits this harness). New query targets are added by writing a single table row.
- Thresholds are **recorded, not asserted**, in the first pass. CI surfaces a regression if a threshold is breached beyond a configured delta (e.g., +25% p95). Initial pass is "land the harness and record baseline"; a follow-up wires it into CI.

### Part 2 — Fix the scan pattern

Minimum viable, in priority order:

1. **Scope by project when the query is scoped.** `BeadsByProject` should call a new `GetBeadSnapshotsForProject(projectID, filters…)` that only opens that one project's store. Trivial change; eliminates the cross-project factor for the common case.
2. **Early filter pushdown.** `GetBeadSnapshots` should apply status/label/projectID filters at the file-parse boundary where cheap, not after materializing every bead. (Still reads every file — deeper fix below.)
3. **Lightweight in-memory index with invalidation.** After (1) and (2), if "all beads" or search is still slow, introduce an index keyed `{projectID, beadID}` with basic metadata (status, labels, title, updatedAt). Refresh on bead write + debounced filesystem watch. Full-body reads stay lazy — the index never holds bodies. This is the same storage discipline we adopted for sessions in `ddx-2ceb02fa`.
